key_insight: Optimizing chemical vapor transport growth conditions can suppress a dominant electrically active defect in CrSBr by up to an order of magnitude, pointing to a sulfur-related vacancy complex as the likely origin of the D* signature.
engineering_relevance: This provides a practical growth-control strategy for improving crystal quality and tuning defect populations in CrSBr, which is critical for reliable magneto-electrical device performance.
method: Correlated CVT growth parameters with defect densities measured by conductive atomic force microscopy (CAFM).
Varied precursor stoichiometry and found sulfur- and bromine-rich conditions reduced D* defect density.
Lowered absolute growth temperatures while keeping the temperature gradient fixed to further suppress defects, supported by thermodynamic modeling and DFT.
evidence_signal: CAFM measurements showed the D* defect concentration could be reduced by up to 10× under optimized growth conditions, consistent with modeling that assigns D* to a sulfur-related vacancy complex rather than an isolated point defect.
watch_item: The defect assignment is model-supported rather than directly atomically resolved, so further microscopic validation may be needed before generalizing the growth recipe across all CrSBr synthesis setups.
paper_title: Growth-controlled suppression of electrically active defects in CrSBr
